focus_ar: الحروف الأنفية والانزلاقية + صوت الشوا + حروف علة جديدة
arabic_challenge: The schwa /ə/ is the MOST COMMON sound in English but doesn't exist as a distinct phoneme in Arabic. Arabic speakers tend to pronounce every vowel fully — Americans 'swallow' unstressed vowels into schwa.

isolation.instructions: /m/ = lips closed, air through nose. /n/ = tongue tip behind upper teeth, air through nose. /ŋ/ (sing) = BACK of tongue up, air through nose. (No /g/ sound at end!)
isolation.instructions_ar: /m/ = الشفاه مغلقة. /n/ = طرف اللسان خلف الأسنان. /ŋ/ = مؤخرة اللسان مرتفعة (مثل نهاية 'سِنْج' بدون g).
practice: ng-ng-ng (back of tongue, like end of 'sing')

sentence_practice: Good morning! I am coming soon.
Nothing is wrong — everything is going well.
daily_drills.record_this: Good morning. Something interesting is happening this evening. I am running — nothing can stop me. I am getting stronger and stronger.
daily_drills.self_check: For /ŋ/ (sing, morning, going): your tongue should NOT touch your teeth. It's the BACK of the tongue. Say 'sing' — do NOT say 'sing-guh' (no /g/ at the end!).

isolation.instructions: /h/ = just a breath of air (like fogging a mirror). /w/ = lips very rounded, then open (like starting to say 'oo'). /j/ = tongue high and forward (like starting to say 'ee').
isolation.instructions_ar: /h/ = نفس هواء فقط (مثل تبخير مرآة). /w/ = الشفاه مدوّرة ثم تفتح. /j/ = اللسان مرتفع (مثل بداية ee).

sentence_practice: Hello! How are you? I want water.
We work with young people every week.
Yes, you are welcome to my home.
daily_drills.record_this: Hello! How are you today? I want to go home with you. We worked hard this week. Yes, you are young and happy.
daily_drills.self_check: /h/ is just air — no friction in the throat. /w/ starts with rounded lips. /j/ (y-sound) starts with tongue high like 'ee'.

isolation.instructions: THE MOST IMPORTANT SOUND IN ENGLISH. /ə/ = short, lazy, neutral — like a tiny 'uh'. It appears in EVERY unstressed syllable. Americans never pronounce unstressed vowels clearly — they ALL become schwa.
isolation.instructions_ar: أهم صوت في الإنجليزية! /ə/ = صوت 'اَه' قصير وكسول. يظهر في كل مقطع غير مشدد. الأمريكان ما ينطقون الحروف غير المشددة بوضوح — كلها تتحول لشوا.
practice: uh uh uh (very short, very lazy, mouth barely open)
Try saying 'a' as fast and lazy as possible — that's schwa

daily_drills.the_rule: ANY vowel letter (a, e, i, o, u) in an unstressed syllable becomes /ə/. The spelling doesn't matter — only the stress matters.
sentence_practice: I want to go to the store today. (to = /tə/, the = /ðə/, today = /tə-DAY/)
A banana and a cup of coffee. (a = /ə/, of = /əv/)
My family lives together in a comfortable apartment. (4+ schwas!)
daily_drills.record_this: Today I want to go to a computer store. My family and I — we live together. About a hundred people support us. It's not a problem.
daily_drills.self_check: Record yourself. Then listen: do your unstressed syllables sound lazy and short? Or are you pronouncing every vowel clearly? The lazier your unstressed syllables, the more American you sound.

isolation.instructions: /ɜːr/ = the American 'R-colored vowel'. Tongue curls back, lips slightly rounded. Like saying 'errr' as in 'bird'. /ʌ/ = short, open, relaxed — like a quick 'uh' but stressed (unlike schwa which is unstressed).
isolation.instructions_ar: /ɜːr/ = صوت R الأمريكي الممدود (مثل bird). اللسان يرجع للخلف. /ʌ/ = صوت 'أَه' قصير ومفتوح (مثل cup).
practice: uh uh uh (short, stressed, mouth slightly open)

sentence_practice: I work hard and learn new words first.
My mother loves her brother. He comes from another country.
The girl turned and heard a bird. It was wonderful.
daily_drills.record_this: I work early in the morning. First, I learn some new words. My mother and brother come from another country. I love them very much — it's a wonderful world.
daily_drills.self_check: For /ɜːr/: is your tongue curling back? You should feel tension in your tongue. For /ʌ/: it's quick and relaxed — don't hold it long.

isolation.instructions: /ɛ/ = mouth open medium-wide, tongue mid-height and front. Like the 'e' in 'bed' — wider than /ɪ/ (sit) but not as wide as /æ/ (cat).
isolation.instructions_ar: /ɛ/ = الفم مفتوح متوسط، اللسان في الوسط والأمام. أوسع من /ɪ/ (sit) بس أضيق من /æ/ (cat).
practice: Compare: ih (sit) — eh (set) — ah (sat) — feel mouth open progressively

sentence_practice: Get ten red pens from the desk.
My friend said breakfast is better when it's fresh.
I never get seven hours of rest in bed.
daily_drills.record_this: I get up and get ready. Then I eat breakfast with my friend at seven. He said he never gets enough rest. I set ten things on the desk every morning. It's getting better.
daily_drills.self_check: Compare these three: sit /ɪ/ — set /ɛ/ — sat /æ/. Your mouth should open MORE with each one. Can you feel three distinct positions?

focus_areas: The schwa in common words: about, today, banana, together, computer
/ɜːr/ in: work, learn, first, bird, world, girl
/ŋ/ endings: morning, going, nothing, something, running
challenge_sentences: Every morning I go to work together with my brother.
Nothing is better than learning something new about the world.
I want to get a computer — it's not a problem.
My friend said something funny at breakfast.
daily_drills.record_this: This morning I went to work early. I learned something new about computers. My friend and brother were together — they said nothing about the problem. Everything is getting better. I never give up.
daily_drills.self_check: Count the schwas in your recording. There should be 10+ schwas in that passage. Are you swallowing the unstressed syllables?

test_yourself.passage: Every morning I learn something new about the world. My mother and brother work together at a computer company. Nothing about it is a problem for her — she is wonderful. I want to get better at everything. First, I turn on my computer. Then I work for about seven hours. In the evening, my family comes together. We never forget to support each other.

sounds_to_check./ə/ schwa: every(ə), about(ə), together(ə-ə), computer(ə-ə), something(ə), a(ə), for(ər), to(tə), the(ðə)
sounds_to_check./ʌ/: something, come, mother, brother, other, company, nothing, wonderful
sounds_to_check./ɛ/: every, get, better, seven, never, together

scoring_guide.90_100: Schwas clearly reduced. /ɜːr/ has tongue retraction. All nasals distinct.
scoring_guide.70_89: Good schwa usage in most words. Occasional full pronunciation of unstressed syllables.
scoring_guide.50_69: Still pronouncing most unstressed vowels clearly. /ɜːr/ sounds flat (no tongue curl).
scoring_guide.below_50: Every syllable equally stressed (Arabic pattern). Need to practice schwa reduction extensively.
